raw_fields.nearby_text_after: Python is both a strongly typed and a dynamically typed language. Dynamic typing means that the type of the variable is determined only during runtime.

NumPy Datatypes. bool_ Boolean (True or False) stored as a byte int_ Default integer type (same as C long; normally either int64 or int32) intc Identical to C int (normally int32 or int64) intp Integer used for indexing (same as C ssize_t; normally either int32 or int64) int8 Byte (-128 to 127) int16 Integer (-32768 to 32767) int32 Integer (-2147483648 to 2147483647) int64 Integer (-9223372036854775808 to 9223372036854775807) uint8 Unsigned integer (0 to 255) uint16 Unsigned integer (0 to 65535) uint32 Unsigned integer (0 to 4294967295) uint64 Unsigned integer (0 to 18446744073709551615) float_ Shorthand for float64. float16 Half precision float: sign bit, 5 bits exponent, 10 bits mantissa float32 Single precision float: sign bit, 8 bits exponent, 23 bits mantissa float64 Double precision float: sign bit, 11 bits exponent, 52 bits mantissa complex_ Shorthand for complex128. complex64 Complex number, represented by two 32-bit floats complex128 Complex number, represented by two 64-bit floats

chunks.content_for_embedding: Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean. Sample mean formula. This formula defines the average value \(\bar{x}\) as the sum of the sample values \(x_i\) divided by the number of values \(n\). It is the standard mean used for central tendency.

content_for_generation.formula_explanation: This formula defines the average value \(\bar{x}\) as the sum of the sample values \(x_i\) divided by the number of values \(n\). It is the standard mean used for central tendency.

chunks.content_for_embedding: Numeric: Spread with Variance. Sample variance formula. This shows the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It is the standard unbiased variance formula for a sample.

content_for_generation.formula_explanation: This shows the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It is the standard unbiased variance formula for a sample.

raw_fields.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance Sample Variance

raw_fields.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance

chunks.content_for_embedding: Numeric: Spread with Variance. Sample variance formula. This gives the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the sample mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It measures spread in the data using the usual degrees-of-freedom correction.

content_for_generation.formula_explanation: This gives the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the sample mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It measures spread in the data using the usual degrees-of-freedom correction.

chunks.content_for_embedding: How do we decide if something is positive or negative?. Thresholded binary prediction. This piecewise rule sets the predicted label \(\hat{y}_i\) to 1 when the conditional probability \(P(y_i=1\mid x_i)\) exceeds a threshold, and 0 otherwise. It is the standard way to turn a probability score into a binary classification decision.

chunks.content_for_embedding: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC. False positive rate definition. This formula defines the false positive rate as FP divided by FP+TN. It also expresses the same quantity as the fraction of all negatives that were incorrectly classified as positive.

content_for_generation.formula_explanation: This formula defines the false positive rate as FP divided by FP+TN. It also expresses the same quantity as the fraction of all negatives that were incorrectly classified as positive.
content_for_generation.formula_latex: \text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{\text{negatives we got wrong}}{\text{all negatives}}

chunks.content_for_embedding: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC. False positive rate formula. This formula defines the false positive rate as the fraction of negative cases that were incorrectly predicted as positive. It is written equivalently as FP divided by FP plus TN, or as the number of negatives we got wrong over all negatives.

content_for_generation.formula_latex: \text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{\text{negatives we got wrong}}{\text{all negatives}}

chunks.content_for_embedding: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC. True positive rate / recall. This formula defines true positive rate, also called recall, as the fraction of actual positives that are correctly identified. It is shown as TP divided by TP+FN, or equivalently “positives we got right” over “all positives.”
